title: Brushed underglaze decoration on bisqueware
subtitle: Painting a design onto bisqueware with commercial underglaze colours before a clear glaze coat
excerpt: Commercial pre-mixed underglazes painted onto bisqueware in a design, then covered with a clear glaze coat and fired to cone 6. The underglaze colour fires to a dense, matte-to-satin result visible through the clear glaze. Suitable for line work, geometric patterns, and painted imagery on bowls, plates, and mugs.
sourceNotes: Underglaze on bisqueware technique derived from first principles.

attrs.body: Underglaze and glaze contain metal oxides; wash hands after use. Fire to the manufacturer's specified cone for food-safe results. Do not use food-contact surfaces from pieces that have not been correctly fired. Ventilate the kiln area during firing.
attrs.title: Kiln safety

content.text: Painting
underglaze
directly onto
bisqueware

gives precise control over where colour goes; unlike dipping, which covers the whole surface. The porous bisque surface absorbs the underglaze in seconds and holds it in place while a clear glaze coat is applied over the top. In the glaze firing, the underglaze fuses beneath the clear glaze and becomes permanent.
Preparing the bisqueware

Wipe the bisqueware surface with a damp sponge to remove kiln dust. Allow to dry. Do not wet the surface heavily; the goal is clean bisque that will absorb underglaze evenly, not bisque that is wet and will bead the colour.
Planning the design

Sketch the design lightly on the bisque with a pencil. The pencil marks burn off in the firing. Keep the design simple for the first try. Geometric shapes, broad stripes, or simple motifs work better than fine detail. The bisque is porous, so it wicks underglaze fast, and fine lines are harder to control than on paper.
Applying the underglaze
1. Apply background colour first

If the design uses a background colour, apply it first with a mop brush across the whole intended area. Stir the underglaze in the jar before loading the brush; pigment settles. Apply two coats, allowing each coat to dry between applications. The colour looks lighter than it will fire; three coats produce the densest result.
2. Add detail with a liner brush

Once the background is dry, add detail colours with a liner brush. Underglaze can be applied over dried underglaze; the layering works similarly to gouache. Dark colours painted over light give better contrast than the reverse.
3. Clean the foot ring

Wipe the foot ring clean of any underglaze with a damp sponge. The foot ring must be unglazed to prevent sticking to the kiln shelf. Wax the foot ring with wax resist after the underglaze is fully dry.
Applying the clear glaze coat
Apply a coat of clear glaze over the underglaze by dipping, pouring, or brushing. The clear glaze must be compatible with the underglaze brand and fired to the same
cone
. A single coat of clear glaze over two to three coats of underglaze is typically sufficient.
What it looks like when it is right

After firing, the underglaze colours appear dense and saturated under the clear glaze layer. The surface has a slight sheen from the clear glaze. Areas of underglaze show as flat, matte-to-satin colour; the clear glaze gives the piece a uniform surface.

items.fix: Apply at least three coats. Always judge the final colour when dry, not when wet. Underglaze lightens a lot as it dries.
items.cause: Too few coats of underglaze applied

items.fix: Apply clear glaze in three brushed coats at right angles to each other; or apply by dipping for a more even result
items.cause: Glaze brushed on in too few strokes, or glaze too thin
items.symptom: Clear glaze coat shows brush marks or uneven texture

items.fix: Use glaze and underglaze from the same manufacturer; fire to the specified cone
items.cause: Clear glaze too thick or glaze overfired; incompatible clear glaze
items.symptom: Underglaze bleeds at the edges in firing
